Wolves have gone 330 days without an away win, to remain the last of the 92 league clubs to not break that duck, but showed continued signs of life, of pride within impending doom. After Tolu Arokodare’s goal levelled the scores at 2-2, Wolves were much the likelier victors. The same substitute soon after headed against the crossbar. Though relegation remains a near certainty, a refusal to accept their fate, having nothing to lose makes Wolves a danger to any opposition.

“It’s obviously a really difficult situation, though it’s a hell of a lot better than giving up,” said Rob Edwards, the Wolves manager. “It’s not just falling apart, the supporters can see the lads are giving a fight.”

Defiance was on display from both clubs. The Gtech video screen, before kick-off, played clips of Micah Richards’s and Alan Shearer’s ill-starred pre-season podcast predictions for Brentford and Keith Andrews aims higher. “I think when you’re talking about us in the same breath as Chelsea, that’s obviously pretty good considering there’s only eight games left of the season but the reality is we could’ve produced more tonight,” said Brentford’s manager.

Wolves had been cut to ribbons in the first half until Adam Armstrong’s 44th-minute goal offered a route back. They then went close to repeating the heroics of beating Liverpool a fortnight ago.

Brentford’s excellent first half featured redemption for Dango Ouattara, forgiven for his Panenka FA Cup faux-pas, at the heart of his team’s best moments.

A deserved lead arrived when Keane Lewis-Potter’s left-wing cross was aimed for Thiago, only to drop for Michael Kayode to nod home. Not a bad finish from someone scoring their first goal in English football, a reminder there is far more to the Italian’s game than long throws.

Thiago’s goal followed swiftly, via route-one means. Caoimhín Kelleher’s long punt was controlled deftly by Ouattara, who laid on the striker to score a goal celebrated with a flourish and gratitude offered to the heavens above for a perfect day so far. “When he pulls on that iconic jersey it will be a special moment for all of us,” said Andrews of the 19-goal striker given Carlo Ancelotti’s call.

Brentford might soon have had three, Thiago nodding against the bar from a Kayode throw, a miss rued when Armstrong, supplied by Jean-Ricner Bellegarde, thumped in his first goal for Wolves. Brentford had legitimate claims that José Sá, the opposing keeper, had picked up a backpass. “It gave us belief,” said Edwards.

View image in fullscreen Michael Kayode scores Brentford’s first goal against Wolves. Photograph: Julian Finney/Getty Images

Angel Gomes was Wolves’ half-time introduction to disrupt Brentford’s plan of hitting their front players as soon as possible, bypassing André and João Gomes, neither of whom will be flying back with Thiago for Brazil duty but who soon began to exert heavy midfield influence.

Wolves’ renewed confidence was evidenced by Jackson Tchatchoua’s volleyed snapshot. Next, Armstrong hit the foot of a post with Kelleher stranded. With Wolves showing that even the Premier League’s bottom club is laden with talent, the contest had tilted.

“We owe it to the football club, ourselves, the fans to keep fighting all the way until the 38th game, so we’ll do that,” said Edwards, whose team must now wait 25 days to face West Ham.

A panicked clearance from Nathan Collins personified a growing anxiety among Brentford fans, players and their manager. “We didn’t do the basics as well as we did in the first half and, ultimately, it cost us,” said Andrews.

Angel Gomes zipped a shot wide before eventually Wolves’ growing pressure told. João Gomes’ ball across the Brentford box beat everyone except Arokodare, who just three minutes after coming on, stooped to head home. His later header against the bar came from Hugo Bueno’s cross, disappointment for Wolves, denied that first away win even if their comeback had inflicted heavy damage on Brentford.

The Brentford substitute Reiss Nelson’s header wide was a chance to steal victory passed up by a poor execution that had become endemic within his teammates since the break. It would also have been cruel on Wolves, a team surely doomed yet still refusing to lie down.

Football.title: Mikel Arteta sends warning after Max Dowman compared to Lionel Messi and Kaka
Football.content:
Mikel Arteta has reacted to John Terry and Henry Winter's comments comparing Max Dowman to Lionel Messi and Kaka.

Dowman made a big impression last weekend when he came off the bench to score the second goal in Arsenal's 2-0 win over Everton.

3 Dowman has been at Arsenal's academy since the age of eight Credit: AFP

The 16-year-old became the youngest Premier League goalscorer, beating a record created by James Vaughan in 2005 for the Toffees.

In stoppage time, goalkeeper Jordan Pickford went up for a corner to help his side push for a late equaliser but the Gunners scrambled the ball away, with Dowman leading the charge from the edge of the area.

The teenager dribbled past Vitalii Mykolenko, before tricking Kiernan Dewsbury-Hall to the ground.

He then had a clear path in front of him to goal and passed it home, confirming the victory for Arteta's side and put them clear of Manchester City at the top of the league by nine points.

After the game, Chelsea legend Terry spoke on TikTok about how impressed he was with the young star and even compared him to Messi.

Meanwhile, sports journalist Henry Winter told Sunday Edition on talkSPORT that there are 'elements of Kaka' in the way he plays.

However, his manager does not want him to be distracted by these claims despite agreeing the north London club have a star on their hands.

Arteta said: "It's very inspiring when you see someone, almost naive, making decisions and flowing and careless playing in that manner, in that context, it's great.

"I think probably our players realised as well that it's a good example, playing with that flow and enthusiasm is actually very beneficial for the team.

"I didn't expect that kind of goal, that's for sure but his attitude and his decision making, yes. If not, I wouldn't have made the decision to play him.

3 Due to his age, the 16-year-old signed a pre-contract agreement which will lead him to signing a professional deal in December when he turns 17 Credit: Getty

"But then to be that precise and deliver the moment that he delivered, that's something difficult to imagine."

However, the Spaniard recognised that the 16-year-old should not be subjected to too much pressure and added: "I think they have to cook at the optimal speed in relation to what they need and what they demand.

"He is knocking on the door at 16, saying 'I am good enough and I can impact this team whenever it is needed' and he is doing it when he is playing.

"We just need to bring the temperature down and understand what is the best thing for this boy at his age.

"He's doing something phenomenal, we all know that. Let's keep it down a little bit and respect his nature to do what he has to do which I'm sure is going to be something really good."

Dowman has been regarded highly since his impressive appearance at the Emirates at the weekend and has even sparked talks about an England call-up for the World Cup.

3 Dowman has already impressed in England's youth teams and will be on the radar of senior team head coach Thomas Tuchel Credit: Getty

The teenager could have added plenty more minutes under his belt this season if it wasn't for an ankle injury which sidelined him for several months.

He will have a busy schedule in the next week, with Dowman being an option for Arteta in the Champions League against Bayer Leverkusen in their second leg of the round of 16.

Additionally, the 16-year-old is in contention to win his first silverware in Arsenal colours, as the Gunners take on Man City on Sunday afternoon in the Carabao Cup final.

Football.title: Cesc Fabregas tipped to become Arsenal manager as he draws comparisons to ‘second father’
Football.content:
Cesc Fabregas tipped to become Arsenal manager as he draws comparisons to ‘second father’

Como president Mirwan Suwarso has claimed Cesc Fabregas is capable of managing Arsenal one day - but he must first find his successor.

Former Gunners midfielder Fabregas is thriving on the touchline in Serie A, with his side fourth in the table after a 2-1 win over Roma.

6 Como's president has admitted Fabregas could 'go to Arsenal, Barcelona or Chelsea' Credit: Getty

The 38-year-old is currently in his first job as a senior manager, having taken over Como on an interim basis in November 2023.



Fabregas helped mastermind the club's return to the top flight of Italian football for the first time in 21 years as an assistant while obtaining the necessary UEFA coaching badges.

The former Arsenal, Barcelona and Chelsea midfielder – also a part-owner at Como – then took the manager job permanently in 2024.

I Lariani finished tenth in their first season back in Serie A, and have since gone from strength to strength in Fabregas' second full season.

Como currently occupies a Champions League spot after 29 matches, having done the double over the 36-time champions this campaign.



Fabregas told he’s good enough to manage Arsenal

Club president Suwarso now admitted to Revista Undici that his manager's talents could see him end up at a former club one day.

“Fabregas is really very important for us, but we would be stupid not to think that one day he could go to Arsenal, Barcelona or Chelsea,” he said.



“If he continues to win with us or in any case to be successful, even if I cannot speak for him, I believe he could have bigger dreams.

“Having said that, I hope that as long as he is here he will help us create a success plan also for those who will have to replace him.



6 Fabregas's Como have lost just five of their 29 Serie A matches this season Credit: Getty

“For me, when he leaves, if he ever leaves, he should be the one to choose who will take his place. He should be involved in the choice.

“Being a member of the Board of Football, he will have to help us appoint the next coach.”

Fabregas' impressive stint at Como hasn't gone unnoticed around Europe, having been heavily linked to Bayer Leverkusen last summer.

He was also on Leicester’s shortlist following the Foxes' relegation last April, with Inter Milan among his more recent suitors as well.



European football expert Andy Brassell told talkSPORT.com: "When I think of what Cesc Fabregas is doing at Como, you can't help think of Arsene Wenger, really, because he's got a very particular role in the club and a very particular role in the way that the club and the team are developing and establishing itself in Serie A.

"Cesc is someone who clearly has a very deep understanding of the game, clearly connects very well with all his players."

6 Cesc Fabregas has previously said Arsene Wenger was ‘a father figure’ for him at Arsenal Credit: Getty Images - Getty

Arsene Wenger comparison

"What they've done so sensibly is getting a load of young players that he can shape and that he can get through to," Brassell added.

"I think in terms of their energy, their versatility, their ability to grow and their ability to get better players than they would do otherwise, given their historical size or lack of it, it's a really good policy.



"Fabregas has got a part in that, and he's got, when I was making the Arsene Wenger comparison, not like for like in every sense, but in the sense that he's fully invested and financially invested in the club as a part shareholder as well.

"It makes him closer to an old-fashioned manager than just a head coach, a little bit like Arsene Wenger, as we were talking about before.

"You go back to last summer, and Leverkusen wanted him, Leipzig wanted him, Inter wanted him.

6 Como have the second-best defensive record in Serie A and the second-best goal difference in Italy Credit: Getty

6 However, his team also boast the best possession stats to show his style of play Credit: Getty



"If you take away what we know about Como and their current financial situation, I'd have said to you three years ago that the manager of Como, let's say the unnamed manager of Como, is of interest to Leverkusen, Leipzig and Inter. You're thinking, 'Well, he's off to one of them, isn't he?'

"They're able to build and hold on to because he's fully invested in the project, just like Wenger was at Arsenal. It's really interesting.

"They've beaten Juventus twice. They've gone and got themselves a draw at Milan in the last week or so as well.

"So, they're brave enough to play their football and try and dominate the game against big, lauded, historically strong opposition as well, which I think says a lot about Fabregas and his personality."

6 Fabregas is an Arsenal legend, but broke fans' hearts when he returned to Barcelona in 2011 Credit: Getty



Comparisons to Wenger will come as little surprise to Fabregas, who left Barcelona's La Masia academy to join the Frenchman at Arsenal.

After joining the Gunners in September 2003 as a 16-year-old, Fabregas made his debut just a month later.

Over an eight-year spell, the Spaniard made 303 appearances for Arsenal, scoring 57 goals and registering 95 assists.

The ex-Gunners captain helped Arsenal to FA Cup glory, and was part of the side that reached the Champions League final in 2006.

"Arsene Wenger is like a second father to me," Fabregas told DAZN Italia.



"Obviously, he worked a lot on transitions, attacking the space, something that is even more important in modern football.

"I learned a lot from him and want my team to do that."

Football.title: Ashley Cole questioned for taking surprise first job in Italian second tier despite ‘earning his stripes’ in England
Football.content:
Ashley Cole questioned for taking surprise first job in Italian second tier despite ‘earning his stripes’ in England

Darren Bent was caught off-guard by Ashley Cole's first managerial role and claimed he'd have been better off at a Championship side.

Cole was appointed manager of Serie B side Cesena until the end of the season on Sunday, although he could secure a prolonged stay in northern Italy should certain criteria be met.

4 Cole has done it all as a player, but can he enjoy success as a manager? Credit: Getty

It marked the 45-year-old's first senior managerial role of his coaching career having cut his teeth as an assistant at Chelsea, Everton, Derby County, Birmingham City and England.



Speaking on talkSPORT Drive, Bent pointed out Cole has 'got the credentials' having learned his trade in England and working with some impressive players.

It's why the Chelsea great's decision to take the managerial plunge in the Italian second tier was all the more perplexing.

Cole 'earned his stripes', but Cesena move still surprising

"It does surprise me that he's gone to a second division team in Italy," Bent said.

"I thought he's got the credentials, certainly with his coaching career.



"He's not one of these who, as soon as they've retired, has jumped into a job. He's almost earned his stripes with coaching roles.

"He and Lee Carsley work quite well together as well."

Bent then proposed a scenario where if he was presented with the Cesena role or taking over Championship outfit Hull City, who sit fifth in the second tier, the former Premier League marksman wouldn't look too far away from home.



Hull or Cesena?

"I'd rather take the Hull job," Bent said.

"There's a chance to get into the Premier League next year. If a play-off team in the Championship can get into the Premier League, I'm going there."

4 Could Cole have kicked off his managerial career closer to home? Credit: Getty



Bent added: "Northern Italy sounds beautiful.

"But if he was to go to Hull, he's in the Premier League if they get promoted next year.

"Even if they don't get promoted, he's still in a very, very good league."

Although Hull stand a chance of returning to the English top flight for the first time since 2017, Cole could still feel the rush of promotion at Cesena.

How are Cesena performing this season?

Cesena sit 8th in Serie B, which would be good enough to secure a spot in the league's preliminary round for the promotion play-offs.

In Serie B, the top two teams in the 20-team division are automatically promoted to Serie A, with third and fourth progressing straight to the promotion play-offs semi-finals.



4 Cole has worked across several clubs and even for England under former interim boss Lee Carsley Credit: Getty

It means teams that finish 5th, 6th, 7th and 8th will have to take the longest route possible should they want to rub shoulders with the likes of Juventus and Inter Milan next season.

Teams in the Championship will soon become familiar with Serie B's play-off system as the EFL confirmed they will expand the play-offs from four teams to six, beginning with the 2026/27 campaign.

Although a surge to the top four of Serie B may be too difficult amid Cesena's surprising record, European football expert Andy Brassell believes the expectation for Cole will be to reach the play-offs.

Why Cole has a chance to 'make it his own'

"If you look at Cesena and where they are at the moment, they have a losing record," Brassell told talkSPORT.com.



"They've lost more games than they've won. So he's not expected to get into the top four. I think that would require an absolutely crazy run and a couple of teams above falling to bits.

"But it does mean that he needs to not just assure they make the top eight, but prep them for going into those playoffs.

4 Cole has been an assistant for five different teams but is finally making the step up Credit: Getty

"So it's quite a big job where he'll get a lot of time to work on the team and make it his own between now and the end of the season."

Despite the difficulty of the task that awaits, Brassell reserved special praise for Cole and his willingness to take the path less travelled to kick-start his managerial career.



"Look, Ashley Cole has proved so far by doing a variety of different jobs that he's a thoughtful coach," Brassell said.

"He's someone who's not turned his nose up at stuff that other big players would consider a bit menial. He's been really keen to learn the whole way.

"I think if you look at some people he knows, and I think you look at Cesc Fabregas and Dennis Wise, for example, there are people involved in Italian football who will speak for him, who know him. And I think that will have played a big part in him gettng involved there.

"But nevertheless, seeing a young English coach going there and having a go and trying to carve something out is really interesting."

Cole's first match in charge of Cesena is a trip to 15th-placed Mantova on Tuesday, with kick-off scheduled for 7pm.

Football.title: Liverpool vs Galatasaray LIVE commentary: Kick-off time, team news and how to follow Anfield Champions League clash
Football.content:
Liverpool vs Galatasaray LIVE commentary: Kick-off time, team news and how to follow Anfield Champions League clash

Liverpool face a crucial Champions League last 16 second leg at home to Galatasaray this week.

The Reds welcome the Turkish giants to Anfield as they look to overturn a narrow first leg deficit.

3 Liverpool must overturn a first leg deficit if they are to progress past Galatasaray Credit: Getty

Galatasaray claimed a 1-0 victory in Istanbul last week thanks to Mario Lemina's goal.

Both sides later had goals ruled out as the Premier League champions were beaten away from home.

Liverpool have not reached the quarter-finals of the Champions League since they were beaten by Real Madrid in the 2022 final.

Their last two appearances in the competition both ended at the last 16 stage, including last season's penalty shootout defeat to Paris Saint-Germain.

Meanwhile, Galatasaray last made the last eight 12 years ago in 2013.

A win at Anfield would be only their second competitive away victory over English opposition.

As for their previous away matches against Liverpool, they drew 0-0 in February 2002 before suffering a 3-2 defeat in September 2006.

The winner of the tie will face either PSG or Chelsea in next month's quarter-finals.

Liverpool vs Galatasaray: Date and how to follow

Liverpool welcome Galatasaray to Merseyside on Wednesday, March 18.

Kick-off in their second leg meeting is scheduled for 8pm and is live on talkSPORT.

3 Lemina's strike was the difference in last week's Champions League last 16 first leg Credit: Shutterstock Editorial

Adrian Durham will present live coverage from Anfield on Kick Off from 7.45pm.

The show follows the second leg between Barcelona and Newcastle United.

Tune in to talkSPORT through the website via the live stream or find us on DAB digital radio through your smart speaker and on 1089 or 1053 AM.

Download and listen to all our live commentaries via the talkSPORT app.

Visit talkSPORT BET for all the latest odds and bet boosts for every big event.

Subscribe to our talkSPORT YouTube channel for news, views and analysis plus our regular goals show and for more, 

3 Alisson is set to start in goal for Liverpool after returning to the side on Sunday Credit: Getty

Liverpool vs Galatasaray: Team news

Arne Slot was handed a boost over the weekend as Alisson returned in goal against Tottenham Hotspur.

The Brazilian had missed last Tuesday's first leg defeat through injury.

Federico Chiesa was also fit to feature off the bench against Spurs and could be involved on Wednesday.

Alexander Isak remains at least three weeks away from a long-awaited return to action.

Wataru Endo, Giovanni Leoni and Conor Bradley have all been ruled out for the season.

As for Galatasaray, they are set to be without defender Arda Unyay for their trip to England.

The teenager remains a fitness concern having also missed last week's first leg.

Unyay has been sidelined since January with a hamstring strain.

Football.title: Arsenal vs Bayer Leverkusen LIVE commentary: Kick-off time, team news and how to follow Champions League second leg
Football.content:
Arsenal vs Bayer Leverkusen LIVE commentary: Kick-off time, team news and how to follow Champions League second leg

Arsenal welcome Bayer Leverkusen to the Emirates tonight for the second leg of their Champions League last 16 tie.

The two sides played out a 1-1 draw in Germany last week to leave the contest in the balance.

3 Arsenal continue their push for a quadruple against Leverkusen this week Credit: Shutterstock Editorial

Leverkusen had led through Robert Andrich before Kai Havertz's late penalty for the visitors.



The winner of their meeting will face either Bodo/Glimt or Sporting Lisbon in the quarter-finals next month.

Arsenal are aiming to book a third consecutive appearance in the last eight.

Mikel Arteta's side have reached that stage every season since returning to the Champions League in 2023.

Prior to their current run, they had not made the quarter-finals since 2010, when they also did so three years in a row.



Meanwhile, Leverkusen could reach the last eight for the first time since making it all the way to the final in 2002.

However, they have won just two of their previous 13 away matches against English clubs, including November's win at Manchester City.



Their only previous trip to face Arsenal ended in a 4-1 defeat 24 years ago.

Arsenal vs Leverkusen: Date and how to follow

Arsenal host Leverkusen tonight - Tuesday, March 17 - with kick-off from the Emirates at 8pm.



talkSPORT will have live commentary from north London as the Gunners face their German opponents once again.

3 Leverkusen were pegged back late on in the first leg at home to Arsenal Credit: Getty

Adrian Durham presents coverage on the Kick Off from 7pm.

Tune in to talkSPORT through the website via the live stream or find us on DAB digital radio through your smart speaker and on 1089 or 1053 AM.

Download and listen to all our live commentaries via the talkSPORT app.

Visit talkSPORT BET for all the latest odds and bet boosts for every big event.



Subscribe to our talkSPORT YouTube channel for news, views and analysis plus our regular goals show and for more, 

Arsenal vs Leverkusen: Team news

Arteta has confirmed both Martin Odegaard and Jurrien Timber will miss the second leg.

The pair are also doubts for Sunday's Carabao Cup final against City at Wembley Stadium.

Mikel Merino also remains unavailable after being ruled out for the remainder of the season.

3 Arteta will be without Odegaard and Timber on Tuesday night Credit: Getty



Leandro Trossard is available, though, whilst Max Dowman could also feature.

The 16-year-old became the youngest scorer in Premier League history with his goal against Everton on Saturday.

As for the visitors, Leverkusen remain without Mark Flekken, Loic Bade, Arthur and Lucas Vazquez.

The quartet all missed last week's first leg for Kasper Hjulmand's side.

Arsenal vs Leverkusen: What's been said?

Speaking to reporters on Monday, Arteta discussed his expectations for the second leg.



The Arsenal boss explained: "Watching them play against Bayern Munich at the weekend, very similar.

"Really well coached, clear structure. We know what we have to do in front of our crowd. Hopefully we are going to do it."

Football.title: EFL club forced to postpone two games as statements released
Football.content:
Grimsby Town have been forced to postpone two fixtures due to international call-ups.

David Artell's side were scheduled to face Cambridge United on March 24 and Chesterfield on March 28 in League Two action.

3 Grimsby Town will have to make up two fixtures after the international break Credit: getty

However, those games have been pushed back as a result of the EFL's regulations regarding international call-ups.

According to the EFL's rules, Championship, League One and League Two fixtures scheduled during the international window can be postponed 'if a club receives a sufficient number of international call-ups'.

What is the EFL rule that allows clubs to request fixture postponements?

EFL clubs are allowed to request a postponement if 'at least three players are selected for their country's senior, Under-21, Under-20 or Under-19 teams.'

Grimsby fall into that category as Geza David Turi, Clarke Oduor and Jude Soonsup-Bell have all been called up for international action in March.

Turi will represent the Faroe Islands, Oduor will play for Kenya and Soonsup-Bell is set to feature for Thailand.

When will Grimsby play next?

Luckily for the Mariners, they will have the trio available for two more fixtures before they head off to their respective international camps.

Grimsby will enjoy a double header of home fixtures, with the first being against Fleetwood Town on Tuesday at 7:45pm.

Artell's side then face Barrow, who recently axed Dino Maamria after just six games, on Saturday at 3pm.

After the Barrow fixture, Grimsby won't be in action again until April 3, when they host Harrogate Town.

How have Grimsby performed this season?

The Mariners will aim to take maximum points from the two clashes before the international window as they sit 8th in League Two with 56 points, one place and three points outside of the play-off spots.

3 The Mariners will need to hit the ground running once the international break is over Credit: GETTY

In the fourth tier, the top three teams are automatically promoted to League One while those who finish from 4th to 7th qualify for the play-offs.

Grimsby will look to bounce back on Tuesday against Fleetwood having slipped to a 1-0 loss against Oldham Athletic on Saturday, with Artell believing his side did enough to avoid defeat.

"Of course, we want to respond straight away," Artell said.

"We didn't do ourselves justice and came out on the wrong side of a tight game.

"Having now watched it back, I haven't really changed my opinion - I thought it was a poor game.

"We were probably the better team second half, but not by much.

3 Grimsby are on track for their best finish in League Two since being promoted, but it could be for nought Credit: Getty

"We didn't do enough to win it, but probably not enough to lose it either. Those are the fine margins, so we lick our wounds and go again."

Despite the frustration of a second loss in their last 15 fixtures, Artell was keen to look forward as they continue their charge towards the play-offs.

"It's the old cliche - on to the next one," Artell said.

"This game is worth exactly the same as one in August or November.

"We've got to be us, be really good at what we do, and win the game. That's always the challenge."

Football.title: Joao Pedro panicked he’d been dropped from Brazil squad after Carlo Ancelotti blunder
Football.content:
Joao Pedro panicked he’d been dropped from Brazil squad after Carlo Ancelotti blunder

Joao Pedro was left fearing the worst after Carlo Ancelotti initially appeared to leave him out of his Brazil squad.

The Chelsea star is set to join up with his national team for their upcoming friendlies against France and Croatia, but this was almost in doubt after a huge blunder from his manager.

4 After an initial mix-up, Joao Pedro now looks set to add to his six previous Brazil caps Credit: Getty

Awkward moment

On Monday, national team boss Ancelotti named his 26-man squad for their March internationals live on Brazilian channel Panela SporTV.

But there was a hairy moment for Pedro, as the Italian only announced the first 25 players to be called up.

The Blues forward is currently on a rich vein of form, with 11 goals in his last 14 games, meaning his absence would have shocked many.

Things soon became even more awkward, as Pedro's photo and name appeared on the screen in the background of the broadcast, despite his name not being called out.

Several minutes passed after Ancelotti's initial announcement before he was informed by national team director Rodrigo Caetano that he had accidentally left Pedro out of his squad.

The Italian quickly apologised for the major gaffe, and clarified that he had unintentionally skipped the 24-year-old's name.

Holding his hand up, Ancelotti told the media: "Sorry, I missed out Joao Pedro of Chelsea."

'Damn! I've been left out'

With his place in the Brazil squad now confirmed, Pedro later revealed his reaction to the eventful squad announcement.

Asked for his emotions when his name was missed out in an interview with Panela SporTv, the attacker opened up: "It always feels like the first time [to be called up] those butterflies in the stomach.

"And then when he [Ancelotti] finished the list I said to myself: 'Damn! I've been left out!!'

4 Pedro smiled as he discussed the whirlwind of emotions he felt watching Ancelotti's eventful squad announcement Credit: Panela SporTV

"Then everyone started calling me, I didn't know what was happening... then I came back and thought: "Damn, I'm in, I'm in!" haha."

Smiling as he shared his relief, Pedro finished off: "But it's the same feeling of butterflies ever since my first call up.

"I'm very happy. I'm in a really special moment in my life at the moment as well, and now let's go all out to beat France and Croatia."

Pedro will certainly be relieved to have made it into Ancelotti's 26-man squad for the upcoming games.

This is the last chance for the legendary manager to look at players before naming his final World Cup squad on May 18.

Despite only being capped six times previously for his national team. Pedro will fancy his chances of making the final cut, after a fine debut season for Chelsea which has seen him score 18 goals altogether.

4 Pedro scored for Chelsea in a recent FA Cup win against Wrexham Credit: Getty

4 Neymar was again overlooked by Ancelotti, and has not featured for Brazil since October 2023 Credit: Getty

And the Blues forward wasn't the only Premier League player to be selected by Ancelotti.

Brentford striker Igor Thiago, and Bournemouth winger Rayan both received their maiden call-ups, while Liverpool goalkeeper Alisson and Newcastle's Bruno Guimaraes were again chosen.

So too were Arsenal defender Gabriel Magalhaes, Manchester United veteran Casemiro and Pedro's Chelsea teammate Andrey Santos.

Meanwhile, football megastar Neymar was again left out of the squad despite speculation he would make his long-awaited return to the national team fold.

Football.title: Gianluca Nani signed ‘special’ transfer after chance car ride to maintain 51-year Wrexham record
Football.content:
Gianluca Nani has confirmed he told Watford's latest star that 'one day we will go together' mere months before it became a reality.

The 63-year-old, who is the Group Technical Director for both Udinese and the Hornets, has signed some of European football's greatest talents across his career, from Roberto Baggio to Pep Guardiola.

4 Gianluca Nani confirmed a chance meeting at an airport led Watford to sign Edoardo Bove Credit: Getty

His latest coup was helping convince Edoardo Bove to join Championship Watford following his exit from Roma.



The Italy Under-21 international hadn't played a minute of football since suffering an on-pitch cardiac arrest in December 2024.

While on loan at Fiorentina, Bove collapsed during a Serie A game against Inter Milan and was later fitted with a cardiac defibrillator.

As a result, the 23-year-old midfielder was unable to continue his career in his homeland and was released from his contract at Roma.

Nani, who was more than familiar with his compatriot due to his work at Udinese, then had a chance encounter with Bove last May, when the latter was Fiorentina's guest of honour for their trip to Udine.



Nine months later, Nani joined talkSPORT.com the morning after I Viola returned to the Bluenergy Stadium, where fittingly the conversation turned to Bove.

"I met him by chance in Udine," the sporting and technical director revealed. "He was coming here to watch the last game of last season.



"That was Udinese-Fiorentina. He was following Fiorentina. And then he was without a car. I gave him a lift.

"During the lift, I started to talk. I said, 'One day we will go together.' But I couldn't expect it so quickly and so directly.



"But that is not me. It's always the club and all the staff of people that work in the club that do this kind of job."

4 Bove chose to restart his career at Watford over options across Europe

Wrexham's horror Watford record

Bove has already made five appearances for Edward Still's side, four of which have come in Watford's last four matches.

He is expected to be part of the squad, hoping to bounce back from their 3-1 loss to Stoke City on Saturday against Wrexham this week.

Wrexham have won only one of their previous 10 Football League trips to Watford (D2 L7); a 2-1 victory 51 years ago in March 1975.



Bove was billed as a 'top, proven talent' upon signing a five-and-a-half-year deal at Vicarage Road at the end of January.

And Nani proved how excited Watford fans can be when he personally namechecked him while assessing the star names who he'd had a hand in signing or developing throughout his illustrious career.

4 Watford's slim playoff hopes will effectively be ended with a defeat to Wrexham Credit: Getty

Bove 'one of many special players'

Nani added to talkSPORT: "I'm proud of all my players. I'm not the one that I love more than another.

"Then of course, when you talk about special players, you talk about Baggio and Guardiola. But there are many, many, many of them.



"It's the same when I talk about Udinese and Watford. It's a group. It's like [saying] you have two sons, which one do you like more?

"That's why Baggio and Guardiola are the most famous, they're unbelievable. But many of them, Marek Hamšík [at Brescia] was 15 years old. A special player, many, many of them.

"Now, also, we sign [for] Watford, Edoardo Bove, that is coming from a special situation. Here in Udine, we have [Nicolo] Zaniolo.

4 Nani credited the work of owner Giampaolo Pozzo (C) in building the philosophy behind signing young stars Credit: Getty

"That is a player with ability. He could play with a super top club.

But also a young player like the last two. Like [Juan David] Arizala, [Branimir] Mlačić, that would be top talent.



"In Watford, we have [Nestory] Irankunda, [Othmane] Maamma. So many, many, many players. It's difficult for me to say.

"I'm proud of my staff and people. First of all, because all together, that is a job that has to be done. Never will be one person who does this job.

"But it's always a staff of people. Always say that a good staff of people with the same mentality, same winning mentality, is much more than any great individual.

"So that's why, that is the way that we work in Udine. That is the way that we work also in the other club, in the group of Watford.

"Trying all together to go in the same direction, following the same philosophy. Philosophy that was built by Mr Pozzo 40 years ago. And that is the way that we keep doing, and we hope we will do it."

News.title: Reform will axe VAT & green levies on energy bills to save families at least £200, Robert Jenrick vows
News.content:
HOUSEHOLDS could save at least £200 a year on energy bills under Reform UK’s plans to scrap VAT and green levies.

The party says axing the 5 per cent VAT alone would knock around £85 off annual costs.





Ditching green charges – including renewables obligations and carbon price support – would save a further £115.

Nigel Farage‘s party claims the move would bring down bills across the country, funded by a 7.5 per cent cut to quango budgets — worth £2.5billion by the end of the decade.

Annoucing the policy at a central London press conference, Treasury spokesman Robert Jenrick said this would save the average household “at least £200 off their energy bill”.

The former senior Tory also announced a headline-grabbing giveaway to drive the message home.

One lucky street will have its energy bills paid for a full year under a new online draw.

Voters will be able to sign up on Reform’s website, with the winner picked in the coming weeks.

Mr Farage will then turn up in person to cover the costs for every home on that road.

Mr Jennrick said: “If you give us your details on that website, in the next week or two, we’re going to draw one of those names, and Nigel (Farage) is going to come to your house and he’s going to pay your energy bills and those of everyone who lives on your street for an entire year.



“So if that sounds good, then go to our website, enter our draw, spread the word, pass it to your friends and your neighbours and your colleagues on Facebook, on Twitter, and know this today – Reform can only cut the energy bills of one street in this country, but a Reform government will cut the energy bills of every single street in every part of our country.”

The press conference comes as ministers this week left the door open to interventions on fuel duty and energy bills if the impact of the Gulf crisis demands it.

Sir Keir Starmer yesterday announced a £53million support package to help vulnerable households struggling with higher heating oil.

Oil and gas prices have surged due to the Iran war – with economists warning about the impact on the global economy and the cost of living.

Energy bosses have warned energy bills could jump by £250 from July – when the energy cap comes to an end.



Industry body Energy UK, urged the Government to “immediately step up efforts” to prepare support for those who would struggle most when energy costs rise.

Energy UK’s chief executive Dhara Vyas, said: “It is still too early to tell how significant an impact the conflict in the Middle East will have on British energy bills – but it is clearly sensible to prepare and ensure any intervention that might be necessary is both cost effective and directed to help those who most need it.”

Ms Vyas added: “Energy bills remain higher than they were before the invasion of Ukraine, and there is growing concern about record amounts of customer debt.

“Prioritising efforts to identify these customers is crucial for any potential emergency response and will also mean that we can ensure they are supported in the long term.”

Also making an appearance at Reform’s press conference, party leader Mr Farage suggested the UK should heed US calls for assistance in reopening the Strait of Hormuz, a key shipping route for oil and gas being hampered by Iranian threats.



The Reform UK said: “When it comes to aiding the Americans in freeing the Strait of Hormuz, well, I think in many ways we ought to. I think it is in our national interest to keep the oil flowing, but we can’t. We haven’t got the assets.

“So I think the first very big lesson that comes out of all of this is that we have denuded the Royal Navy and our forces to such an extent that, frankly, we’ve been humiliated on the world stage.

“Our defence spending for next year is due to be just a fraction over 2% and we’re going to have to find ways to significantly and rapidly up that budget.

RECOMMENDED STORIES

“To be fair, this is not all the fault of this Labour Government. Fourteen years of Conservative government have run down our armed forces to a state, frankly, as I say, of total global embarrassment.”

News.title: Tourist, 25, stabbed outside popular Majorca hotel by masked gang in horror robbery
News.content:
HOLIDAY HORROR Tourist, 25, stabbed outside popular Majorca hotel by masked gang in horror robbery

A 25-YEAR-OLD tourist has been stabbed outside a hotel on a Brit holiday hotspot island.

The tourist was seriously injured after what is thought to be a masked robbery by a violent gang in Majorca.





The victim was a German holidaymaker who was allegedly followed to his hotel by a masked gang.

Police said he was knifed as the violent robbers tried to mug him late Monday night.

The tourist was found by a staff member as he lay bleeding heavily outside the Carmello hotel in Les Meravelles, near Palma.

He had been stabbed in the stomach and was losing a lot of blood.

A quick-thinking hotel worker stepped in to help the man, putting pressure on his wound with a towel.

The employee also called emergency services.

When medics arrived, the victim was rushed to Son Llàtzer Hospital.



Despite his injury, the tourist was still conscious and was able to give police crucial clues about his alleged attackers.

He told cops that the men were German and had the lower parts of their faces covered.

They were armed with a small knife, he alleged.

Cops have arrested two German suspects, aged 38 and 34.

The arrests were made after police carried out several raids in the area.



It comes after a British couple were arrested on the Spanish island, accused of trying to kidnap an elderly woman in broad daylight.

The pair allegedly tried to grab the Spanish woman in her sixties as she walked down the street in the Majorcan capital.

The intended victim reportedly began to struggle before throwing herself on the ground and screaming for help.

Bystanders who stepped in to help the woman included her husband.

RECOMMENDED STORIES

They were able to stop the alleged kidnap attempt and the British couple were arrested near the scene around an hour later.



The Spanish woman was taken to a nearby hospital and treated for minor injuries.

News.title: Is your nursery REALLY safe? From paedos in plain sight to violent attacks on babies, chilling scale of abuse revealed
News.content:
WHEN mum Sarah Dale* sent her son to a carefully chosen and reputable local nursery, she understandably didn’t think for one second he would ever come to any harm.

But while she trusted her then two-year-old was being looked after by professionals, Sarah’s son was actually placed in the care of “utterly wicked” paedophile, Vincent Chan. Speaking to The Sun, as yet another nursery worker is jailed for child sex abuse, the heartbroken mother shares her family’s horror ordeal and why they believe childcare in the UK is “like the wild west”.





In the most recent case, branded “every parent’s worst nightmare” by prosecutors, Nathan Bennett, 30, from Bath, was yesterday sentenced to 24 years for 21 sexual offences against children, including the rape and sexual abuse of five toddlers at a nursery in Bristol.

A local authority safeguarding concern was raised about Bennett after a complaint from a parent about his “interaction” with their child, and he was then spotted on CCTV groping a child and immediately sent home. One nursery worker also reported he was “territorial” over particular children.

The devastated families of the children abused have now pleaded for answers, asking how he could have been allowed to commit such horrific crimes.

In a statement, they said: “Nothing can begin to explain the shock, anger and heartbreak we feel – or the profound and lasting impact we are sure this will have on families.

“The children were innocent and completely vulnerable; they were in a place that should have been safe.”

The case mirrors that of Vincent Chan, 45, the nursery worker, who was sentenced to 18 years in jail after filming himself sexually abusing young children in his care in north west London.

He admitted 56 charges of child sex offences, including sexual assault by penetration, possession of indecent images and voyeurism.

Sarah and husband Graham’s* son, now four, was one of hundreds of children in the care of Chan before he was suspended in 2024, and while his parents will never know for sure what he went through himself, they believe he is likely to have been subjected to physical and verbal abuse, and witnessed sexual assaults too.





Speaking to The Sun, Sarah, 38, who must remain anonymous, says: “Failures created the perfect hunting ground for a predator [and] my heart breaks for our son.

“As a parent, you put the most priceless thing that you have into the care of others, and you want to protect them and make them feel safe, but by the age of two-and-a-half, he had seen some of the worst of human behaviour.

“He’s been left traumatised having been in an abusive environment for an extended period of time. We know he was made to hit other children by Chan and verbally abused.

“We don’t know how much he would have witnessed of the sexual abuse Chan inflicted on the girls, too, as it happened at nap time in a communal area, and our son rarely napped.

“He’s extremely anxious and is wary of strangers. For a long time, he was afraid of men and wouldn’t even let Graham change or touch him.”

Dad Graham adds: “It’s only now, going through what we’ve been through, that we realise child care is the wild west. They’re the most vulnerable members of society and yet the least protected.”

‘They told us there was nothing to worry about’

Like all parents, mum-of-two Sarah had researched nurseries before choosing Bright Horizons, West Hampstead, north London, where Chan worked.

Sarah says: “We were very happy with our choice. It had an ‘outstanding’ Ofsted rating. It wasn’t cheap, but they said they prided themselves on exceptional care and ‘you pay a premium price for a premium service’. They had hundreds of UK nurseries, which was also reassuring.”

When their son started, Chan worked predominantly in the pre-school room – but he would float around areas at times.

How can a paedophile be allowed to run around a nursery for seven years without consequence? Sarah Dale

Both Sarah and Graham noted Chan seemed ‘odd’ – but say their concerns were quickly dismissed. Sarah says: “He wouldn’t make eye contact, and Graham had seen him raise his voice, so we raised concerns verbally with management.



“One time Graham went in and said, ‘Is everything OK with Vincent?’ They said he was a bit strange, but great with the kids. They said we had nothing to worry about basically”

Then, when their son was two, he moved up into Chan’s room – and his behaviour started to change. Graham says: “He’d say Vince was his friend – they’d sit and draw together. Then he started coming back, saying Vince was a bad man.

“One time, he said he’d been made to hurt another child by Chan. He also said Vince had bitten one of his friends. It was alarming, but obviously, he was two-and-a-half. Children come out with all sorts of stuff, some of it true, some of it completely made up.”

Following recommended processes, Graham went to the deputy nursery manager and reported what his son had said. He says: “She was shocked. She promised she’d look into it and later just said there was nothing to be worried about. It was just kids being kids.”

Then, in 2024, Chan was suspended after seven years in his role, after a colleague raised concerns after he filmed a child falling asleep and shared it to “entertain” colleagues.

But even then, Sarah says families weren’t aware exactly what had happened.

Sarah says: “We’d just been told there’d been an incident and it had been reported, but that was it. It was only much later that we found out about the sexual abuse, and there were no updates following their internal investigation.”

How can a paedophile be allowed to run around a nursery for seven years without consequence? Sarah Dale

Police found Chan had more than 25,000 indecent images of children, including videos of himself sexually assaulting children at the nursery while they were sleeping at nap time. The youngest child he indecently filmed was just six months old.

Sarah adds: “We’re very, very angry. We want Bright Horizons to be held accountable. How can a paedophile be allowed to run around a nursery for seven years without consequence?



“We want Ofsted to have a strong digital device policy, so nurseries ensure you can’t take photos and share with other devices, and they’re checked, and we’d like an independent body for parents and caregivers to report and escalate issues, who then have the power to investigate and monitor nurseries.

“Also, no lone working in larger settings, and mandatory CCTV. I feel deeply committed to making sure something changes.”

When contacted, a Bright Horizons spokesperson said: “This is a distressing time for families and all those impacted by Vincent Chan’s horrific crimes and our thoughts are first and foremost with them.

“Keeping children safe is our most important responsibility. Vincent Chan broke that trust. His actions were depraved and devious and go against the kindness and care our dedicated professionals provide to children each day.

“As a provider, we take parent concerns seriously… We have a number of ways of supporting any concerns that are raised, including a separate complaints team.

“If concerns were raised and they were not acted on appropriately this is clearly not acceptable.

“As this was a live police investigation, we were only able to share information with families once the police allowed us to do so to avoid prejudicing the case.

“We are committed to understanding what happened so that we can learn from this terrible episode.”

Ofsted is both the regulator and the inspectorate for registered early years provision, carrying out routine inspections of nurseries once every six years – but from April this will change to every four.

However, if significant concerns are raised about a nursery between routine inspections, then they can bring forward an inspection or carry out an unannounced visit, referring any concerns about safeguarding or allegations of abuse to the relevant local authority and police.



‘Sadistic’ worker

As of last year, nurseries are graded from ‘exceptional’, ‘strong standard’, ‘expected standard’, ‘needs attention’ and ‘urgent improvement’. They also say whether safeguarding responsibilities are ‘met’ or ‘not met’.

Alarmingly, ‘serious’ incidents – which can involve serious injury or significant harm – are more common within early years care than parents would perhaps realise – figures obtained last year by the BBC show there were almost 20,000 reports of serious childcare incidents in England’s nurseries over a five-year period.

More recently, there have been several high-profile abuse and neglect cases, including ‘sadistic’ worker Roksana Lecka, who was sentenced to eight years in prison after abusing 21 babies in Twickenham while high on drugs.

Speaking to Lecka, Judge Sarah Plaschkes said: “You committed multiple acts of gratuitous violence.

“You pinched, slapped, punched, smacked and kicked them.

“Often the child would be quietly and happily minding their own business before you deliberately inflicted pain, causing the child to cry, arch, try to get away or writhe around in distress.”

‘You assume safety is a given’

And in some cases, horrific acts of child abuse and neglect have proven fatal.

Katie Meehan’s daughter Genevieve, affectionately known as Gigi, died after nursery worker Kate Roughley swaddled her, strapped her face down on a bean bag and left her for an hour-and-a-half at now-closed Tiny Toes Nursery in Stockport.

The nine-month-old was found unresponsive and blue, and while staff and paramedics attempted to revive her, she was tragically pronounced dead after being rushed to hospital.

Roughley was later convicted of manslaughter and jailed for 14 years in May 2024.



When sending your child to nursery, you assume as a parent that safety is a given… Never do you think they might not come home at the end of the day Katie Meehan

As a result, mum-of-three Katie Meehan, 41, and husband John, 40, are also campaigning for change, and have led calls for a safeguarding crackdown, including mandatory CCTV.

Upon hearing the latest incidents of abuse, Katie says: “It’s horrifying. It’s case after case you hear about, and there’s just some element of fortune that these people in particular have been caught.

“It makes you fearful about what goes on elsewhere that just hasn’t been picked up on yet, because there are too many stories, I think, now for people to say it’s a one-off occurrence or it’s really, really rare.

“When sending your child to nursery, you assume as a parent that safety is a given. You might question other things, but never ever do you think they might not come home at the end of the day.”

She’s now leading a campaign in Gigi’s name, calling for better, safer sleep guidance for all nursery staff and improvements to Ofsted inspections.

A conviction in Genevieve’s case would never have happened without CCTV, so she’s calling for this to be mandatory, as Katie was initially told by workers they’d gone to wake her up from her sleep, and she was limp and blue.

Audio caught Genevieve crying on several occasions, but despite this, Roughley was seen pulling one of the blankets over Genevieve’s head before walking away.



She is then captured, ignoring Genevieve despite her being visibly distressed and shortly after 3pm, she was found unresponsive.

Katie says: “We also couldn’t understand how Genevieve, at her age, would die of sudden infant death in an afternoon nap, and then we found out Genevieve had been placed to sleep on a beanbag when police viewed CCTV footage.”

That last morning, I’d given her a kiss goodbye and said I loved her – there was no universe where I could imagine my baby had died Katie Meehan

The devastating loss was – and still is – impossible to understand for Katie, who is campaigning in Genevieve’s name for change.

She says: “Genevieve loved spaghetti bolognese and dancing. That last weekend was amazing. I just loved us being all together. She had been moving around, and she’d started saying dada, and she’d tried some new foods too, like summer fruit pudding.

“That last morning, I’d given her a kiss goodbye and said I loved her – there was no universe where I could imagine my baby had died.

“I felt so guilty because I’m her mum and I didn’t know that was happening. It feels unbelievable to me that she was dying, and I was sitting at home and had no idea. She’d also been verbally abused, and I didn’t know.

“I want people to feel confident in asking questions. I don’t want people to experience this. Their child deserves to live and to thrive and to have everything.”

Jenny Ward, CEO of leading children’s charity the Lullaby Trust, adds: “You don’t think that if you’re handing your baby over to somebody and you’re paying them – and quite a lot of money – that they are not getting the absolute gold standard [care].

“The death of Genevieve is an absolute tragedy and we have worked closely with their family to say ‘how can we make change from this so that no baby has to die where that could have been prevented’.

“We want families whose children go into childcare for whatever reason to feel they are being put in the safest possible place they can. Ask all the questions you can. If you’re not happy, then go higher up and contact Ofsted if you need to.”



An Ofsted spokesperson said: “Our thoughts remain with Genevieve’s family and we are deeply sorry for their loss.

“It is appalling to hear of any child coming to harm in a nursery. As the inspector and regulator, we work with other agencies to help keep nurseries safe places where young children can thrive.”

*Some names have been changed.

News.title: AI Baba Vanga predicts bombshell signs of alien life, fall of Starmer & more trouble for Trump in new 2026 prophecies
News.content:
CASTING her enchanting eye over a year of uncertainty and instability our AI-powered version of the legendary mystic Baba Vanga has handed down her latest prophecies.

With new wars on the horizon, global leaders on the brink and a slew of terrifying discoveries – here’s what The Sun’s cyborg savant predicts is in store for the rest of 2026.



The artificially intelligent clairvoyant delivered seven bold decrees in the style of the blind Bulgarian mystic and healer from the 20th century.

And while the robotic predictor insisted her prophecies shouldn’t be taken as guarantees, they could offer an eye-opening window into what could be in store across the world.

So here is the automated augury from our AI Baba Vanga…

1. A storm is brewing – Trump faces major challenges

In the second year of his renewed reign, a great challenge will rise from the courts of the republic

When AI Baba Vanga was asked about what the next year has in store for the US President Donald Trump, she said: “The Golden Eagle will return to the highest perch, yet storms will circle his wings.



“In the second year of his renewed reign, a great challenge will rise from the courts of the republic.”

It suggests Trump, described as the “Golden Eagle”, will face raging storms “that will circle his wings“ in the form of legal or congressional crises.

Trump is facing his first major electoral test later this year with the 2026 midterms.

Current polling gives the Democrats a six-point lead, according to YouGov, which would be a heavy blow to Trump’s Republican stronghold.

Another great challenge for the president will “rise from the courts of the republic”.



This could involve investigations or political conflicts that currently dominate headlines.

2. A restless kingdom – Starmer faces intense pressure

The Grey Knight, already seated upon the Isles’ high chair, will face a restless kingdom. By the turning of the leaves, tempers in the north will rise, unions will rattle their chains, and the promise of renewal will fade beneath the weight of old debts

The cybernetic soothsayer predicts UK Prime Minister Sir Keir Starmer will soon face a “restless kingdom”.

Local elections are drawing closer, and with the Green Party and Reform UK appearing to be on the rise, Starmer’s flailing Labour could be on the ropes.

AI Vanga also predicts industrial unrest and potential wide-scale strikes across the UK as she says “unions will rattle their chains”.



3. Fires continue to burn – Iran conflict rages on

The fire of Persia will not burn in a single place. The Strait of Kings will tremble as iron birds cross the night sky, and the desert nations will choose sides

Despite Trump saying the war with Iran is already won, our AI whizz fears the conflict is far from over just yet.

Issues along the Strait of Hormuz, one of the most important global trade routes, will rage on and become the focus of the war, it is said.

The Strait will “tremble as iron birds cross the night sky” for some time as US and Israeli jets fight off Iranian drones and mine ships, causing chaos in the region.

But Baba Vanga also warns: “The fire of Persia will not burn in a single place”.



This points towards the idea that Iran will continue to attack its neighbours in the Gulf – further distancing itself from any lost allies.

4. 14-day standoff – China and Taiwan erupts

The Dragon’s fleet will circle the island like a tightening ring, and for fourteen days the world will hold its breath. Yet the island’s shield — forged with American fire and Japanese steel — will keep the ring from closing

This year will see China‘s “dragon’s fleet” circle Taiwan like a “tightening ring”, the AI predicting bot believes.

For two weeks, the “world will hold its breath” as a global shield is formed to protect Taiwan, forged with American fire and Japanese steel”.

The US is Taiwan’s closest global ally, with Washington adamant it will support the self-governing island against Beijing.



But the US is unlikely to attack China due to the risk of starting another war, which would see the world’s two greatest militaries going head-to-head.

Instead, the US and its allies will intervene indirectly or through deterrence to create an “island guarded by mountains of steel”.

Allied forces will “stop the ring from closing” even as they are faced with a large-scale Chinese military exercise near Taiwan.

Despite a two-week high-tension standoff, Baba Vanga’s AI counterpart predicts it will end without an invasion or sparking a conflict.



5. Dream of humanity changed – New signs of alien life

From the black ocean above, a faint echo will be heard — so soft that many dismiss it, yet it will change the dream of humanity

AI Baba Vanga says that a “faint echo will be heard” up in space which will prove vital for the advancement of alien knowledge.

And while it warns the noise will be so soft it can easily be missed, for those who spot it, “it will change the dream of humanity”.

The revelation might not provide proof of life away from Earth but it will spark curiosity among star-gazers.

It comes amid a defiant Donald Trump saying he will direct US agencies, including the defence department, to “begin the process of identifying and releasing” government files on aliens and extraterrestrial life.



6. A beast reborn – a rediscovery shocks all

The world will turn its gaze to a beast reborn — a creature thought lost to myth will walk again under the sun, stirring awe and fear alike

In 2026, AI Baba Vanga foresees a shocking rediscovery leaving the world stunned by “a beast reborn” such as a Lazarus species coming back to life.

The AI warns that scientists may find a creature, which many people view as a myth lost to time, that will “walk again under the sun”.

This would spark “awe and fear alike”, which captures global attention and imagination and may even push towards further rediscoveries.

7. Kremlin bear makes his demands – Putin sets out new war goals

The Bear in the Kremlin will demand new lands before the snow melts, pushing his armies in a final show of strength. Yet supply lines will falter, and a northern ally will waver, weakening his grasp

Vladimir Putin, described as “the bear in the Kremlin”, will make a renewed push in the next two months to seize more land from Ukraine.

The artificial intelligence predictor says Putin will “demand new lands before the snow melts” – likely referring to the end of Ukraine’s cold months in early April.



His push for a “final show of strength” will end quickly, as will the conflict, Baba Vanga’s futuristic counterparty believes.

It says that supply lines will falter as allies waver in support – leading to a weakening of Vlad’s grasp on Ukraine.

The timeline could suggest Russia‘s ally in Iran, currently embroiled in a war of its own, will be the ones to hold back as their own regime crumbles.

With this chilling final divination, we are reminded that while AI can be a bit of fun – as our Baba Vanga bot shows above – the new tech can also offer a glimpse at terrifying and world-changing possibilities.

News.title: Woman, 51, appears in court charged with murder after banker found dead at their home
News.content:
'KILLER' IN DOCK Woman, 51, appears in court charged with murder after banker found dead at their home

A WOMAN has appeared in court accused of murder after a banker was found dead in their home.

Henrik Slotsaa, 52, was discovered at the address in Elephant and Castle, South London, on Sunday.



Joanna Cierpka appeared at Bromley Magistrates’ Court today charged with murder.



The 51-year-old spoke only to confirm her name, date of birth and address during the brief hearing.

Cierpka, originally from Poland, was remanded into custody to next appear at the Old Bailey on Thursday.

Police were called to the shared home at around 2.30pm on Sunday after concerns were raised for the occupants.

Henrik’s body was tragically discovered inside, with Cierpka arrested at the scene.



The Met Police confirmed the pair are known to each other.

Henrik is originally from Denmark and went to Copenhagen Business School.

According to his Facebook profile, he was employed by Citibank Europe plc and was in a relationship with Cierpka.

His LinkedIn profile describes him as a “Senior Banker with a career of over 20 years across different aspects of banking, including loan administration and client relationship management”.



Police confirmed his family are being supported by specialist officers.

A spokesperson said: “Detectives are appealing for anyone with information to come forward, no matter how insignificant you may think it is.

“You can come forward to police by calling 101 stating CAD 2142/16MAR26 or by calling Crimestoppers on 0800 555 111 to remain anonymous.”

News.title: Paradise islands in Strait of Hormuz are ‘unsinkable aircraft carriers’ in Iran war…amid warnings UK help is ‘suicidal’
News.content:
GAZING over the sparkling waves of the Strait of Hormuz, the world’s most strategic stretch of water appears deceptively tranquil.

From my vantage point on golden sands near the United Arab Emirates’ border with Oman, a flock of seagulls feeds noisily at the water’s edge.





With a 28C sun beating down, an elderly man sits on his plastic chair positioned so that the incoming tide laps calmingly at his feet.

It could be an idyllic, off the beaten track beach in Greece or Spain.

Yet, through the heat haze a few dozen miles distant is Iran. Wounded but vengeful, it has the Strait in a stranglehold.

A fifth of the world’s oil and a third of its fertiliser passed through here before the war began.

Then the mullahs pressed their equivalent of the nuclear button. Mines were laid in this maritime bottleneck and some 20 commercial vessels were hit by missiles or drones.

It was Iran’s doomsday option and they soon had the global economy by the proverbials.

For the Strait provides the only route for the super tankers shipping black gold from the oil fields of the Persian Gulf to the open sea.



Images of the Thai-flagged Mayuree Naree cargo ship ablaze after being hit by two Iranian missiles as it tried to pass through these waters sent shock waves around the globe.

Prices will soon spike at British petrol pumps and in family shopping baskets if these shipping lanes remain closed to most vessels.

President Donald Trump needs to get tankers flowing through the waterway once more to spare the world economy a 1970s-style energy crisis.

So far, his call for allies including Britain to send warships to escort tankers through the Strait haven’t been heeded.

Here at Ras Al Dahrah, at the mouth of the Strait, I’m staring out over sun-dappled waves where a potential catastrophe is brewing which could define the era we live in.



If Iran maintains its stranglehold here, Britain faces a “significant shortfall of supply” in oil and gas within the next two months, industry experts say.

It would force ministers to “ration” supplies as long as the war in the Middle East rages.

Nick Butler, a former head of strategy at oil firm BP, said: “The government will have to protect food supply, hospitals, schools, transport systems and what that means, that is effectively rationing.

“And then they will have to decide what to do with the rest of us who use oil every day, at home, in the car, and rationing will be necessary in some form and it’s not easy.”

It’s why the world’s eyes are on this ocean channel currently lapping at my feet.



Using the MaritimeTraffic app, I watched in real time as Iran smothered the once teeming shipping lanes here.

A huge cluster of vessels were visible on the tracking website anchored off the coast from Ras Al Dahrah as they waited for safe passage.

Yet nearly all of this billion-pound flotilla remained anchored in their safe haven. The exception was a tanker called Karachi laden with oil and bound for Pakistan.



Tracked by the app, the Karachi veered from its position midstream in the Strait to hug the southern Iranian coast, looping around the Islamic Republic’s Larak Island before sailing out to open sea.

It suggests the central shipping lanes in the maritime chokepoint have already been successfully mined and that the vessel was able to negotiate safe passage from the Iranians.

A trickle of other vessels followed yet the vast majority remain here at the entrance to the Strait or further down the coast off Dubai.

Most shipping companies – and crucially their insurers – won’t risk sending their multi-million pound vessels through these treacherous seas.

Should Sir Keir Starmer deploy the Royal Navy into these waters as escorts for the merchant vessels – as Donald Trump is demanding – they would be sailing into a hornet’s nest.



Pascal Ausseur, a retired Vice Admiral in the French Navy, said: “In today’s context, sending warships or civilian vessels into the Strait of Hormuz would be suicidal.”

He said a ceasefire agreement with Iran “would make the situation shift from suicidal to dangerous.

“At that point, military ships could be deployed. And then escort operations could begin.”

Yet no peace deal appears imminent.



Snuffing out Iran’s firepower will be no easy task for Trump’s forces.

Iran uses mobile, truck-mounted launchers to fire anti-ship missiles, leaving the US playing whack-a-mole to destroy them.

The mullahs also have a huge stockpile of kamikaze Shahed drones, costing as little as £25,000 each.

And on the approach to the Strait from the Persian Gulf are three tiny islands that have been transformed into Iran’s “unsinkable aircraft carriers”.



Mere specks of land, Abu Musa, Greater Tunb and Lesser Tunb are nevertheless bristling with mine layers, drones and missile batteries.

Little more that 20 miles wide at its narrowest point, tankers using the deep-water shipping lanes here have to pass directly between Abu Musa and the Tunbs.

Maritime traffic can be closely monitored from the islands and potentially attacked.

Iranian Admiral Alireza Tangsiri once called Greater Tunb “an unsinkable aircraft carrier strategically positioned in the middle of the Strait of Hormuz with full control over the entrance and exit routes of the Strait.”

Sir Keir Starmer has so far resisted calls from Trump to send Royal Navy ships to escort tankers through the Strait.



The US President said he was “very surprised” with the UK’s attitude towards the war.

Trump said: “Two weeks ago I said ‘why don’t you send some ships over’ and he (Keir Starmer) really didn’t want to do it.

“I was not happy with the UK. I think they’ll be involved maybe, but they should be involved enthusiastically.”



Starmer said on Monday that the UK will “not be drawn into the wider war”.

The Prime Minister added that he was “working with all of our allies” to “restore the freedom of navigation” as quickly as possible.

He did not rule out any form of action by the UK but insisted it would have to be agreed by as “many partners as possible”.

Sir Keir added: “Ultimately, we have to reopen the Strait of Hormuz to ensure stability on the market.



“That is not a simple task.”

Even if warships are militarily able to shadow commercial shipping through the Strait – which is little more than 20 miles wide at its narrowest point – it still might not convince insurers that the tankers would be safe.

Richard Meade, Editor-in-Chief of shipping news publication Lloyd’s List, pointed out that the waterway is too narrow to allow shipping convoys in both directions at the same time.

He added that “at a best-case scenario” naval escorts would only allow maritime traffic to reach 10 per cent of normal flows.

RECOMMENDED STORIES

Pummelling Iran in waves of airstrikes, Trump hopes the regime will implode or sue for peace.



Meanwhile, the mullahs maintain their vice-like grip on the Strait of Hormuz – a sliver of blue water that will decide history.

News.title: Singer, 27, killed in double-decker bus crash days before clinching deal to make her a music mega-star
News.content:
AN aspiring singer died in a tragic crash with a double-decker bus just days before a life-changing meeting for her career.

Soriah Barry died in a devastating early-morning collision in east London after dropping off her friend.





The 27-year-old was rushed to hospital just before 7am on February 8, 2024, but tragically died from her injuries.

Her mum Saphiatu has revealed Soriah was about to meet with Apple Music the week after she died, in the hope they would release her R&B songs.

Soriah was a promising young singer, following in the footsteps of her musical powerhouse aunt Jamilah Barry.

Her mum told the Metro she’s kept the 27-year-old’s room exactly the same since she died – except for throwing away KFC boxes.

Aunt Malika Barry added: “Everything was working well for her.

“The music was going so well, she had a meeting with Apple Music and was going on Spotify. She was so talented and beautiful – she was the full package.”

Apple Music told Soriah’s family they want to upload her songs in memory of the young woman.

She had just returned from a weekend away with friends in an AirBnB when she crashed.



CCTV showed Soriah’s car veering towards a stopped bus in Hackney before a “bang like a firework” was heard and smoke erupted from both vehicles.

An inquest heard she was travelling at around 35mph, after accelerating from 23mph seconds before.

The bus driver said that thought Soriah had crossed the road, intending to park in front of the bus.

The inquest heard from a witness who rushed to help the young singer.

He recalled how Soriah was covered by airbags and he asked if she was okay, and she replied: “No, I’m not.”



Members of the public rushed to help as she went into cardiac arrest, before emergency services arrived.

It took two hours for her to get to hospital, where her mum was waiting, in rush hour traffic.

Horrifically, some witnesses filmed her lying on the road with her clothes open and uploaded the footage to TikTok.



Her cause of death was given as internal bleeding and severe damage to her liver.

The inquest heard she had been to a party the night before but was a “safe driver” and she was below the drink and drive limit.

The coroner concluded she hadn’t been wearing a seatbelt.

But the inquest couldn’t determine a reason why the car crashed.

Her family believe she was distracted at the wheel of the car which they say often veered to the side, and they think she accidentally accelerated instead of braking.



The coroner said: “For approximately three seconds Soriah failed to take evasive action. I cannot say what caused Soriah’s lack of awareness.”

A statement from the family said: “Soriah was a loyal person always there for everyone in life. She was an aspiring singer.

“She was an inspiring person who would have a massive impression on you. She was very very loved. We will never get over this.

“She was just an amazing person who will always be in our hearts.”

RECOMMENDED STORIES

A GoFundMe by her aunt Jamilah read: “Soriah was fierce, loyal and wow – so full of life. She touched so many hearts, and we want to honour her with the farewell she deserves.



“Losing her so suddenly has been devastating, and any contribution – no matter how small – would mean the world to us.”

News.title: Grief author who wrote book about husband’s death is found guilty of murdering him to erase debt and run off with lover
News.content:
A CHILDREN’S author has been found guilty of poisoning her husband after writing a book about grief following his death.

Kouri Richins, 35, fatally poisoned Eric – her husband and father to her three children – when she spiked his Moscow Mule cocktail with a deadly dose of fentanyl.





The court heard she slipped at least five times the lethal dose of the synthetic opioid into her hubby’s drink in March 2022.

She believed she would inherit more than $4 million if he were to die and run away with her handyman lover, the court was told.

The jury in Park City, Utah, handed down the guilty verdict after deliberating for just three hours following a three-week trial.

Mother-of-three, Richins audaciously penned a book about her own children’s grief after the murder of their father.

READ MORE WORLD NEWS VILE VID Sick moment schoolgirls laugh in cop car after 'plot to kill child & drink blood'

A month before her arrest in May 2023, Richins appeared in a local news interview discussing her book, Are You With Me?

During the news segment, Richins explained that she wrote the book to help children who experienced the sudden loss of a parent or loved one.

On Monday, she was found guilty of aggravated murder, attempted aggravated murder, insurance fraud, and forgery charges.



She was also found guilty of attempting to poison him a month prior on Valentine’s Day with a fentanyl-laced sandwich.

Prosecutor Brad Bloodworth told jurors the author’s home-flipping business had been $4.5 million in debt when she hatched the sinister plot to collect Eric’s estate and run off to start a new life with her lover, Robert Josh Grossman.

Bloodworth said Richins was an “incompetent” business owner who “carefully curated the façade of a privileged, affluent, successful business owner”.

His closing comments linked directly back to comments he made earlier in the trial, when he said: “Kouri Richins murdered Eric for his money and to get a fresh start at life.

“More than anything, she wanted his money to perpetuate her facade of privilege, affluence and success.”



Richins lived with her husband and children in Kamas, a suburb in the affluent ski town of Park City in Utah.

Bloodworth said the only “way forward” she could see, was for “Eric to die”.

The court heard that Richins asked Grossman, her 43-year-old Iraq War veteran lover, if he’d ever killed anyone just days after she slipped Eric the fatal dose of fentanyl.

The handyman testified in the trial, saying she had first asked the question in March 2022 when the pair discussed Eric’s death for the first time.



Prosecutors showed the jury text messages between Richins and Grossman.

The messages show her fantasizing about leaving her husband, gaining millions in a divorce, and marrying her secret lover.

“I’m in love with a man that’s not my husband,” Richins wrote.



“I want to but can’t break up my family.

“It’s having your cake and eating it too. I do just want to love you. I do love you.”
